content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 6, 2019

Taylor Kovach
Deane & Company
1787 Tribute Rd, Suite K
Sacramento, CA 95815

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-220

Dear Taylor Kovach:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Sacramento City Council candidate Lesley Simmons regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May Sacramento City Council candidate Lesley Simmons pay a $650 invoice for hair and makeup services for a campaign photoshoot using campaign funds?

CONCLUSION

Yes. As the photoshoot was undertaken to create campaign literature, the expenditure of campaign funds are directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Lesley Simmons is running for the City Council of Sacramento in 2020. He received hair and makeup services for a photoshoot for campaign literature, invoiced at $650.

ANALYSIS

In general, the "personal use" of campaign funds laws are designed to prevent candidates, elected officials, and others who control the expenditures of campaign funds from benefiting privately from their campaign activities. As such, any expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-220
Page No. 2

minimum, reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose associated with a
candidate's office. (Section 89512.) If an expenditure confers a substantial personal benefit on a
candidate with a value of more than $200, the expenditure must be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512(a).)

You inquire whether a $650 invoice charged to Mr. Simmons for a campaign literature
photoshoot may be satisfied using campaign funds. Because the expenditure confers a substantial
personal benefit on Mr. Simmons with a value of more than $200, the expenditure must be directly
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. Under the facts provided, the
expenditure was a one-time occurrence and for the direct purposes of creating additional campaign
literature to be used for the duration of the campaign. There is no indication that the single
expenditure was intended to offset Mr. Simmons regular personal expenses. Accordingly, the
expenditure as described is directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose, and
an expenditure of campaign funds to pay for these services is permissible. Please be advised that
our advice is limited to the specific facts provided in this request and should not be construed to
permit the use of campaign funds to make payments for hair and makeup services under any other
circumstances.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: [signature] Ryan O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

makeup services for a campaign photoshoot using campaign funds?

CONCLUSION: Yes. As the photoshoot was undertaken to create campaign literature, the expenditure of 
campaign funds are directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
